title: Leveling Up Your Company: A lexicon for digital transformation success
summary: Leveling Up Your Company: A lexicon for digital transformation success Over the course of fifteen years and hundreds of customer deployments, we at Palantir have had some time to reflect on what …
content:
Top highlight

Leveling Up Your Company: A lexicon for digital transformation success
Palantir
Follow
9 min read
·
Nov 22, 2019

490

4

Over the course of fifteen years and hundreds of customer deployments, we at Palantir have had some time to reflect on what drives impactful digital transformation — and there’s even more learning left to do. But what we do know is that digital transformation is a journey, not a destination; and when done right, businesses should see impactful operational improvements every step along the way.

We’ve developed a framework to help organizations evaluate their progress along the journey, using our experience to help tackle the obstacles at every turn. In this post, we’ll walk through the five levels of enterprise autonomy, explaining the methodology and milestones critical to becoming a truly data-driven company. Join us as we level up the conversation about digital transformation.

Press enter or click to view image in full size
The five levels of enterprise autonomy
Asking the right questions

So where do you start? Our clients typically approach us with one of two conundrums: they’ve successfully implemented a data lake, but they haven’t realized any business impact from the technology; or they’re struggling to deploy their investment in artificial intelligence or analytics in way that tangibly impacts their operations. In our view, the first step is to define the right goals, and then use technology to accomplish them.

For example, prospective clients often ask if we can help them better predict customer churn. We can reliably predict who’s going to churn and when — even up to eight times more accurately than chance. But now that we know who’s going to churn, what do we do? How do we keep them from leaving? Simply predicting churn is very different from preventing it. You might find that a strong predictor of churn is a customer invoice dispute; but, by time your model catches this predictor, it’s too late to save the client. Similarly, you may find that a strong predictor of churn is the customer living in a country that’s entered a recession — but what action can you take with this information? Leaning in to complexity — the thousands of decisions that go into servicing a customer during their lifecycle — offers the greatest opportunity for value, and a source of sustainable competitive advantage. If you can use data to examine and optimize every customer touchpoint, from the customer service interactions and product marketing suggestions, to invoice disputes and more, you can take action before churn is even on the table.

Whether they’re consumer companies or heavy manufacturers, our clients’ businesses are becoming more complex every day. In our experience, the key to starting off strong is embracing this complexity. Our software manages complexity by integrating data and systems in a robust, repeatable, and secure way. We help organizations leverage the universe of decisions that their operators make every day to accomplish their goals.

Starting your journey

To fully harness the power of your data, you first need to unify it. Typically, we’ll have our clients start by targeting one specific decision and identifying the relevant data sources needed to make the decision. This involves breaking down interdepartmental silos, connecting to a variety of source systems, and making previously-hidden data queryable in a central place.

Press enter or click to view image in full size
At level 0, data is queryable in a central place, but it’s still pretty inaccessible to anyone without a data science background.

However, at this level, the data is still pretty inaccessible to anyone without a data science background, which makes it insufficient to actually begin solving the problems you’ve surfaced. Moreover, many institutions struggle with enabling access for non-technical users, as well as questionable data quality and perplexing data governance. This is the data lake we hear so much about

title: Net Zero is a Data Integration Problem: How Palantir enables data-driven decarbonization
summary: Palantir is helping companies achieve net zero goals through data-driven decarbonization, aligning long-term goals with day-to-day operations.
content:
Net Zero is a Data Integration Problem: How Palantir enables data-driven decarbonization
Palantir
Follow
5 min read
·
Mar 25, 2021

291

1

In 2020, organizations in every sector set or strengthened net zero targets. Manufacturers plan to minimize non-renewable materials along complex supply chains; financial services firms aim to align their portfolios with sustainable investment practices; and governments have put budgets towards a green recovery from the pandemic.

Multi-decade net zero goals are laudable, but they need to be backed up with credible decarbonization pathways. In 2021, the challenge is to align long term goals with day-to-day operations. Decarbonization requires fundamental changes. Decision makers need a detailed, data-informed understanding of operations to make the changes that, in turn, make net zero a reality.

In this blog post, we’ll look at why some common approaches to decarbonization can limit progress. If you’re interested in deploying Palantir Foundry to support your net zero efforts, reach out to us.

Press enter or click to view image in full size
Carbon calculators miss the details

These are the typical steps for setting a net zero target:

Estimate aggregate emissions for the organization
Determine aggregate reduction targets
Work backwards from those to set sub-targets for each year and business group

To estimate emissions, many organizations use a carbon calculator. The logic of carbon calculators is simple: they multiply a quantity of an activity with the carbon coefficient for that activity. If employees collectively fly 500,000 miles in a given year, and the carbon coefficient for air travel is 150 g per passenger-mile, the aggregate emissions are 75,000 kg of CO2e. A global manufacturer, for example, would multiply carbon coefficients by quantities for activities relating to supply, manufacturing, distribution, and sales, and sum the totals.

But like multi-decade net zero goals, carbon calculators are low-fidelity. Aggregates and estimates make it impossible to track emissions back to the processes that caused them. The result is an incomplete record of climate footprint that can’t be broken down for analysis.

When it comes to building a detailed pathway to net zero, the aggregate emissions numbers from carbon calculators aren’t that helpful. To achieve net zero, organizations need to track their emissions in a granular way, expose them to operators, and link them to day-to-day decisions.

Get Palantir’s stories in your inbox

Join Medium for free to get updates from this writer.

Subscribe

To give an example: a shipping planner has been asked to create a lower emissions route for a climate-focused customer. The planner has no way to see how her decisions affect the emissions of routes under her control. Instead, she has to make assumptions from existing information, like speed, cost, and vessel utilization. This makes it difficult to develop robust low-carbon products, and means that she may miss potential optimizations.

Press enter or click to view image in full size
Palantir lets shipping planners visualize the carbon metrics of shipping routes. All data is notional.

Another example is executive compensation: several organizations have announced that they will link bonuses to sustainability metrics such as carbon emissions, alongside routine performance markers like margin and growth. This is a powerful and important adjustment of incentives. But unlike financial metrics, most business leaders have no way to discover and evaluate the emissions under their control. There is no detailed accounting and reporting structure to help them pinpoint high emissions sources and identify low-hanging fruit for improvement.

Net zero is a data integration problem

Since our inception, Palantir has enabled organizations to develop pathways for complex business transformations. Our approach to net zero goals incorporates some lessons we’ve learned along the way:

High-level metrics don’t allow

title: Product Design at Palantir: Q&A with the team
summary: In this blog post, Product Designers share why they joined Palantir, their day-to-day schedules, advice for potential applicants, and more.
content:
Top highlight

Product Design at Palantir: Q&A with the team
Palantir
Follow
8 min read
·
May 19, 2022

24

1

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Product Designers are key members of Palantir product teams. We work on Gotham, Foundry, and Apollo platforms alongside Product Managers and Engineers to define product vision and user experiences. Using design as a communication tool, we ensure that all products shipped convey our mission and reflect our exceptional standards. Product Designers are a force multiplier, helping drive massive impact in product direction and business outcomes, from Palantir’s work with vaccine distribution to counterterrorism.

Get Palantir’s stories in your inbox

Join Medium for free to get updates from this writer.

Subscribe

In this blog post, Product Designers share why they joined Palantir, their day-to-day schedules, advice for potential applicants, and more.

Why did you choose to join Palantir?
Andy E. (Denver, US)

The initial thing that drew me in from the beginning, and now eight years later, was the huge variety of different types of problems within incredibly important domains, everything from national security to healthcare. I still find myself being surprised and intrigued by problems that Palantir is solving. Besides, Palantirians are incredibly humble, driven, and phenomenal critical thinkers. It has really been amazing and humbling to be able to work alongside my teammates here and I’ve learned so much from them.

Jiyoung O. (Palo Alto, US)

I found Palantir while doing my graduate school thesis on how design can help navigate complex messages, systems, and technologies. I was first attracted by the complex problems that the company deals with and how the products help important institutions use technologies like AI/ML. After watching Palantir’s Gotham and Foundry demo videos and seeing how design contributed to these workflows, I knew Palantir would be a perfect place to apply what I’ve learned!

Charles P. (London, UK)

I joined Palantir because I wanted to serve people that rely on software to perform some of the world’s most critical workflows. I wanted to bring the high UI standards, which consumer-facing products often have, to mission-driven products and, by doing so, transform the way users understand and interact with software in critical moments. And I knew I could do that at Palantir. In addition, I joined early in my design career and was looking for a place with opportunity for mentorship from talented designers, as well as autonomy.

Aashman G. (New York, US)

I was initially drawn to the complexity and breadth of the problems I would be able to solve at Palantir. I enjoy working within a relatively flat structure — the best opinion wins, and you are able to forge your own trajectory in the company regardless of your role. Lastly, I was excited to work with an autonomous and multifaceted group of people every day.

What’s a typical work day like for you on the design team?
Chanel L. (New York, US)

I’m often designing for a few projects at a time, each at different phases. For projects that are in implementation phase, I stay up-to-date with teams’ priorities and plans so that I can provide feedback and iterate quickly with my developer teammates. For these projects, the design quality is held at a very high bar. Other projects are in much earlier stages and, in these cases, I act as a strategic partner alongside the product managers, tech leads, and other designers to create vision designs and draft concept primitives to guide discussions.

Candice J. (Palo Alto, US)

I’m mainly responsible for our geospatial analysis tool on the Gotham platform, and therefore I often think about translating large scale data sets into human-readable information on a map. A typical day for me is comprised of meetings with my team, including engineers and product managers, design presentations, solo design time on projects big and small, as well as progress documentation.


[...truncated]

title: Products with Purpose: Foundry — Archetypes
summary: Palantir Software Engineer Julien shares how Foundry Archetypes is driving successful outcomes for customers when timelines matter most.
content:
Products with Purpose: Foundry — Archetypes
Palantir
Follow
6 min read
·
Oct 5, 2021

15

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Julien is a Software Engineer at Palantir, currently working on one of the products within our Foundry platform. We sat down with Julien to learn more about the product and his own journey at Palantir.

Hi Julien, thanks for joining us today. Could you tell us a little bit about who you are and what you do at Palantir?

Sure! I’m Julien, my pronouns are he/him, and I am currently a Software Engineer working on Foundry in our London office. I joined Palantir in 2017, and during these last four years, I have worked as a developer on different products across the Foundry platform, in product management, and as a Forward Deployed Software Engineer embedded with customers. (Editor’s note: You can learn more about the difference between our Software Engineer, or Dev, roles and our Forward Deployed Software Engineer roles in this prior blog post.)

What brought you to Palantir?

Before Palantir, I had varied experiences both as a software engineer and as a strategy consultant. As a software engineer, I enjoyed the technical and productized approach to solving problems, and as a consultant, I valued seeing the impact I could have on broad real-world problems. Palantir was the best of what had excited me in both, providing me the ability to work on problems that have a real-world impact, while creating scalable technical solutions that could help our products profoundly improve operations for multiple customers.

You work on one of our flagship platforms, Foundry. Can you tell us a little bit about the platform?

At a high level, Foundry is a platform that allows our customers to manage their own data, create models that represent their organization, and operationalize data to actually help drive decisions. The goal is not to have a shiny analysis that remains on a shelf in a deck; Foundry actually changes how our customers operate and helps them make decisions in the real world, where the situation may be constantly evolving.

Foundry can be adapted for a wide variety of customers and use cases, because the widgets, modules, UI, and configurations can all be customized. Foundry provides the infrastructure to easily build the logic, models, and apps that power operational outcomes at different levels of an organization. This infrastructure includes:

Data syncing: How do you read data from the customer’s system?
Data transformation: How do you clean up and transform that data to a useful format?
Ontology: How do you make that data understandable, so you’re not just talking in terms of tables and rows, but in terms of real-world objects that those tables and rows actually represent?
Modeling: How do you manage the models that represent the cause and effect relationship between those entities?
Enablement: How do you build apps so that people can actually interact with those objects and drive real-world outcomes?

This is a high-level overview of what Foundry does right now, but Foundry is constantly evolving to provide more value for our customers. Because of our operating model, our Forward Deployed Software Engineers are able to give the Product Development team direction about insights and trends they’re seeing first-hand in the field. This direction drives a lot of our product work within Foundry, and it’s actually how the product I’m currently working on came into existence.

Can you tell us more about the product you’re currently working on?

In early 2020, we noticed that many of our customers, government and commercial alike, were experiencing similar problems — namely, how to track the impact of COVID-19 on their facilities, people, or supply chains. Our customers needed to be able to understand and measure the impact the pandemic was having on their business in real time. So we created a product called Archetypes, which provides workflow templates that can be deployed from a configuration wi
[...truncated]

title: Requirements for AI in Production in Insurance Underwriting
summary: Learn how insurers can deploy AI in production in accordance with, and anticipating, the most rigorous legal, regulatory, and ethical considerations.
content:
Requirements for AI in Production in Insurance Underwriting
Palantir
Follow
17 min read
·
Feb 7, 2025

39

1

Press enter or click to view image in full size
Introduction

Large language models present both massive opportunities and significant complexities for the insurance industry. Insurers can use AI to increase operational efficiency, improve the accuracy of underwriting decisions, enhance customer experience, and more effectively coordinate with partners. Yet in a heavily-regulated industry like insurance, ensuring objectivity and the appropriate level of human oversight in policy underwriting is legally and ethically crucial, and governing AI at scale requires management and orchestration.​
​
Insurers face increasing scrutiny about how they plan to deploy AI — scrutiny that will carry profound legal and financial implications. For example, the National Association of Insurance Commissioners Bulletin on Use of Artificial Intelligence Systems by Insurers states that insurers are expected to adopt practices, such as governance frameworks and risk management protocols, designed to ensure that the use of AI systems does not result in unfair practices. [1] ​

As of January 3, 2025, 21 jurisdictions have now formally adopted this bulletin. Regulatory and law enforcement agencies will carefully examine how insurers use AI to make decisions or support actions that impact consumers. ​
​
Through our extensive experience partnering with several major industry institutions grappling with these challenges, we have developed a set of best practices that we believe are crucial for enabling insurance institutions to deploy AI in production in accordance with, and anticipating, the most rigorous legal, regulatory, and ethical considerations.​
​
In this blog post, which uses underwriting as an example core insurance business application, we provide an outline of these best practices, organized according to the following core themes: ​
​
1. Understandable — AI results must be interpretable and understandable.​

2. Integrated — AI must be deeply integrated with existing business systems and processes.​

3. Governed & Secure — AI must be securely governed and controlled at scale. ​

Insurers seeking to introduce AI must protect themselves by making their AI-based processes explainable, traceable, and auditable. And those that master these capabilities now will turn AI governance from a regulatory burden into a strategic weapon, seizing a decisive competitive edge.​

Deploying AI for Underwriting

AI within underwriting can enable underwriters to make better, faster and more consistent decisions. In addition to helping to reduce operational costs, it can help advance key underwriting KPIs, such as quote-to-bind ratios, gross/net premium, renewal retention, and ultimately, impact the loss ratio. It can enable better risk selection and vetting through improved data extraction and research (e.g., human rights considerations), facilitate fluid communications with partners, increase speed to decision, and support underwriter due diligence. And it can enhance integration with the deal lifecycle, from initial quote to closing.​
​
More specifically, insurers can deploy AI within underwriting across the following steps: ​

Data Extraction: LLMs can be used to rapidly extract relevant data from submissions + attachments by detecting relevant fields within unstructured data, PDFs, loss runs, broker emails, etc.​

Research Due Diligence: LLMs can be used as a research assistant, pulling in relevant information for the underwriting decision from internal or external sources. This could be by mapping extracted data to internal historical data (e.g., this submission is linked to an existing policyholder), or by researching across any number of external sources (e.g., this person or company requesting insurance was recently involved in a particular news story). ​

Exception Alerting: LLMs can be used to generate alerts for underwriters by automatically detec
[...truncated]

title: Tips on finding great jobs
summary: Tips on finding great jobs Fall of 2018, I applied to and interviewed with many tech companies in Seattle: everything from satellite offices of established companies to a two-person startup. Through …
content:
Top highlight

Tips on finding great jobs
Bryan Hanner
Follow
17 min read
·
Feb 19, 2020

195

1

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Fall of 2018, I applied to and interviewed with many tech companies in Seattle: everything from satellite offices of established companies to a two-person startup. Through the process that ultimately led me to Palantir, I assembled a job candidate’s toolbox for applying to companies, staying organized, and interviewing. I explain my process and considerations in the hopes that fellow job seekers can find a few takeaways that resonate with their own process.

Apply

When I approached the gargantuan task of finding a post-graduation job, I chiseled away at the work by starting early. Both in the sense of starting long before I wanted to find a job, and setting aside time to prepare for each unique conversation or interview along the way. That’s the major tool that helped me keep taking feedback and course-correcting as I met with companies. Personally, I started applying to roles about three months before I wanted to have everything decided, and I applied to Palantir halfway into that period. Touching up my resume for those first few applications and blowing the dust off talking about my work at DroidCon NYC got me in the mindset for what the next few months would look like. Once I was knee-deep in interviews, I’d make sure I had breathing room between events every day to nourish myself (get food and refill the water bottle), look over notes, and reset my mind from whatever I just came from. I could usually read through all my previous notes about a company before each time we’d talk.

Now, I want to point out that each application process will look different, even over time for a single person. It’s often a classic bootstrapping problem: I’m looking for a job but I haven’t done this job before, so how do I prove myself? There is no simple solution, but do not be discouraged if you get rejections or don’t hear back at first! Volunteering or working on your own projects are great ways to build your resume until other opportunities come your way. Many companies like Palantir will look beyond traditional experience and consider a wider variety of folks too. For instance, in a sea of no’s, I only had one interview the first cycle I applied for internships, and the conversation started because of my theater experience of all things. That ended up being my first internship.

As you explore the job market, keep in mind what you are prioritizing, measured by your time investments. I personally found success refining my resume and always answering the short answer questions in applications but seldom writing a traditional cover letter (very few were required). My Palantir application was an example of this, though since they provided a text box for their cover letter I wrote a short message there (cover letters can be short!). That being said, a cover letter might be extremely effective for you. I encourage you to tell your own story through your application and tweak how you present it based on what resonates with companies. More generally, if you find what pays off the most and invest time in that, you can choose the game you’re playing and play it well.

For my resume, I found success by returning to what a Google recruiter told me over my college years: “Quantify what you’ve done. We want to see what impact you’ve made: how many people you reached, how much money you saved, and so on.” While endeavoring not to sound like a robot, I did include hard numbers on my own resume when I could. In fact, one line was particularly inspired by this: “FAQs [what I was working on] will be used by 4,000+ service agents & 100,000+ customers across 3+ companies”. Even giving an order of magnitude communicates the scale you worked at, and just as importantly shows you’re thinking about scale in the first place.

The most important timing question is the first one in a dialogue with a company: when should y

title: Understanding ROI with Palantir Foundry
summary: With Palantir Foundry, leaders can get a real-time view of their projects, and better measure ROI for more effective future strategy.
content:
Understanding ROI with Palantir Foundry
Palantir
Follow
5 min read
·
Jun 20, 2022

34

Press enter or click to view image in full size

These days, it’s nearly impossible to imagine a road trip without a mobile phone and navigation app. While printing out a guidance route or referring to a paper map seems so archaic to us today, we forget that these error-prone methods were the only options we had just a few years ago. How did we know when we’d get to our destination without knowing the traffic conditions lying ahead? What did we do when we took a wrong turn, or when the roads themselves had changed in the time since our map on hand was printed?

In many ways, many companies today still use the equivalent of paper maps for navigating their financial and human resources. At Palantir, we’ve built the next-generation navigation platform for our finances, helping our people understand where they are today and see what lies ahead.

For many leaders, the process of understanding where their companies are investing and how those investments are faring involves weeks of work from internal teams and outside consultants, due to siloed, sensitive data and fragmented organisational views. The data likely live in different systems altogether, and may include sensitive information that must be handled carefully or only for certain purpose. The best version of the resultant output is an accurate picture of a company’s state when the process started. The faster a company grows and the surrounding world changes, the more outdated the decisions made from this picture are.

To avoid unpleasant surprises when it comes time to report, whether at the end of a quarter to shareholders or to ad-hoc investor meetings, many companies must resort to implementing hurdles designed to avoid spending outside of a rigid top-down structure. While this helps limit damage in some realms, it may inhibit organizations’ abilities to respond to change and even take advantage of investment opportunities.

This is a technology problem.

Why is getting a real-time view of your expenses and revenue hard?

Let’s extend the metaphor just a little further. To help you traverse the physical world, your mobile phone’s navigation app needs to integrate data from a variety of sources ranging from GPS satellites orbiting earth to traffic information gathered from myriad databases. The task of compiling a real-time-view of your company’s operational picture and comprehensive ROI involves a similar set of challenges:

Data is spread across databases that don’t talk to each other. From financial reporting tools, such as expense and billing management tools, to more task-specific tools like Google Ads or AWS billing, financial data and corresponding understanding of ROI are dispersed across systems. In order to understand a project’s performance, expenses must be married to other metrics, which likely live in different systems altogether: e.g. travel, payroll, and revenue data.
The standard organisational view is fragmented. Each of the above-mentioned tools comes with its own, distinct data model, and sits at a unique vantage point within the organisation. The view from that vantage point does not often provide a holistic picture of how the overall organisation wants or needs to operate. This impedance mismatch can force the organisation to alter its structure towards one dictated by those tools, just so that the data provided will make sense. This can make it difficult to change organisational structures as needed, because the barrier to changing the corresponding external financial systems is too high.
Financial data is often an organisation’s most sensitive data. Ensuring accuracy and appropriate access to financial data poses a delicate technological challenge where a mistake can have market-moving implications. The effort and complexity involved with disseminating this information can be a significant limiting factor to the organisation’s ability to diffuse decision-making ac
[...truncated]
